--- Page 1 ---
1
Robust Cooperative Output Regulation of
Discrete-Time Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Systems
K¨urs¸ad Metehan G¨ul and Selahattin Burak Sarsılmaz
Abstract—This article considers robust cooperative output reg-
ulation of discrete-time uncertain heterogeneous (in dimension)
multi-agent systems (MASs). We show that the solvability of
this problem with an internal model-based distributed control
law reduces to the existence of a structured control gain that
makes the nominal closed-loop system matrix of the MAS
Schur. Accordingly, this article focuses on global and agent-
wise local sufficient conditions for the existence and design of
such a structured control gain. Based on a structured Lyapunov
inequality, we present a convexification that yields a linear matrix
inequality (LMI), whose feasibility is a global sufficient condition
for the existence and design. Considering the individual nominal
dynamics of each agent, the existence is also ensured if each agent
solves a structure-free control problem. Its convexification yields
LMIs that allow each agent to separately design its structure-free
control gain. Lastly, we study the relationships between the sets of
control gains emerging from both global and local perspectives.
Index Terms—Cooperative control, distributed control, struc-
tured control, output regulation, linear matrix inequality (LMI),
Lyapunov methods, discrete-time, multi-agent system.
I. INTRODUCTION
A. Literature Review
The extension of the robust output regulation problem [1]–
[3] to multi-agent systems (MASs), known as the robust
cooperative output regulation problem (RCORP), provides
a unifying framework to formulate and solve cooperative
control problems, including leader-following consensus [4],
formation tracking [5], and containment [6]. For continuous-
time homogeneous (in dimension) [4], [7] and heterogeneous
(in dimension) [8]–[11] linear MASs over general directed
graphs, RCORP has been well studied by leveraging the
distributed internal model approach. The synthesis of control
gains for internal model-based distributed control of hetero-
geneous MASs can be grouped into two: global design [8]
and agent-wise local design [9]–[11]. A global design method
treats the closed-loop MAS as a single system, imposing
a particular structure on the overall control gain, especially
zeros for numerous entries. Such structured control synthesis
problems are believed to be NP-hard [12]. On the other hand,
an agent-wise local design method focuses on the individual
agent dynamics, allowing each agent to design its structure-
free control gain independent of other agents.
One computationally efficient approach to address struc-
tured control problems is to restrict solutions of the Lyapunov
inequalities to specific structures [13], [14]. The existence of
K¨urs¸ad Metehan G¨ul and Selahattin Burak Sarsılmaz are with the
Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering, Utah State Uni-
versity,
Logan,
UT
84322,
USA
(emails:
kursad.gul@usu.edu,
burak.sarsilmaz@usu.edu).
such structured solutions has been studied for a few structures
[15]–[17]. Specifically, in [16], a necessary and sufficient con-
dition for the existence of a diagonal solution to the algebraic
Riccati equation is derived, whereas sufficient conditions for
the existence of a block-diagonal solution to Lyapunov and
H∞Riccati inequalities are presented in [17]. Though in
general it remains an open question which is less conservative,
another computationally efficient way is to pass the structure-
imposing to the new matrices through a characterization of the
Lyapunov inequalities based on the projection lemma [18].
Compared to its continuous-time counterpart, RCORP for
discrete-time linear MASs is seldom addressed. For homo-
geneous (in dimension) MASs, the nominal closed-loop sys-
tem matrix is similar to a block upper or lower triangular
matrix whose ith diagonal block consists of nominal system
parameters of agent i and an eigenvalue induced by the graph
matrices [19]–[21]. However, the nominal closed-loop system
matrix does not enjoy this useful property when the MAS is
heterogeneous in dimension. In this case, to establish agent-
wise local design methods, [22] derives small-gain solvability
conditions, and [23] employs a distributed observer that pro-
vides each agent with an estimate of the exogenous signal.
B. Contribution
This article considers the RCORP of discrete-time uncertain
heterogeneous (in dimension) linear time-invariant MASs over
general time-invariant directed graphs with an internal model-
based distributed dynamic state feedback control law, which
does not exchange controller states of neighboring agents. This
control law solves the RCORP under standard conditions if
there is a structured control gain, with a particular structure,
that makes the nominal closed-loop system matrix Schur (see
Theorem 1 and Remark 2). Though the stabilizability of the
pair of interest, established in Lemma 5, is necessary for
the existence of such a control gain, it is not sufficient in
general (see Example 1). Accordingly, this article presents
global and agent-wise local sufficient conditions for the ex-
istence and design of the structured control gain. Based on
a structured Lyapunov inequality, the feasibility of a linear
matrix inequality (LMI) provides a global sufficient condition
for the existence and design (see Lemma 6, Remark 3, and
Theorem 2). The existence is also ensured if each agent
solves a structure-free control problem (see Theorem 3). Its
convexification yields LMIs for each agent’s control gain
synthesis (see Remark 6 and Corollary 1). Therefore, the
proposed global and agent-wise local design methods can be
conducted in polynomial time.
arXiv:2603.07783v1  [eess.SY]  8 Mar 2026


--- Page 2 ---
2
In terms of scalability and conservatism, we briefly compare
both design methods. The global design method simultane-
ously synthesizes each agent’s control gain, whereas the agent-
wise local design method does so separately. Thus, the agent-
wise local design method is much more scalable. Section VI
investigates the relationships between the sets of control gains
emerging from both perspectives. One of the results is that the
global design method is less conservative than the agent-wise
local design method (see Corollary 3 and Example 3).
This article differs from the relevant studies [22], [23] in
the following key aspects. The proof of the solvability result
in [22] enforces stability of the nominal local dynamics of
each agent, disregarding the case in which the closed-loop
system matrix of the MAS is Schur with unstable nominal
local dynamics. In contrast, the solvability result in this article
does not disregard such cases (see Theorem 1 and Example
5), and hence, it is more general. While [22] provides an
agent-wise local perspective on the existence and design of the
structured control gain, this article establishes both global and
agent-wise local sufficient conditions, with the local conditions
implying the global one. In particular, the proposed agent-wise
local sufficient conditions construct a Lyapunov inequality for
the nominal closed-loop system matrix of the MAS, which is a
useful tool for handling switching graphs [24], event-triggered
control [25], and bounded parametric uncertainties within the
context of the continuous-time RCORP. Lastly, the distributed
control law in [23] necessitates a communication network for
the MAS since agents exchange their controller states, whereas
the considered distributed control law in this article can still
be applicable in the lack of a communication network since
agents use their relative outputs with respect to their neighbors,
which can be measured by their onboard sensors.
C. Notation
We write 1n for the n×1 vector of all ones, In for the n×n
identity matrix, 0n×m or 0 for the n × m zero matrix, ⊗for
the Kronecker product, and diag(Xi) for the block-diagonal
matrix whose entries on the main diagonal are Xis for all i
in the corresponding index set. The vector formed by stacking
columns of a matrix X ∈Rn×m is denoted by vec(X). The
spectrum and determinant of a square matrix X ∈Rn×n are
denoted by spec(X) and det(X), respectively. Let Sn
++ denote
the set of n × n symmetric positive definite matrices. For
symmetric matrices X and Y , X ≻Y (⪰, ≺, ⪯) means X−Y
is positive definite (positive semidefinite, negative definite,
negative semidefinite). We write λmin(·) and λmax(·) for the
minimum and maximum eigenvalue of a symmetric matrix.
Lastly, the cardinality of a set S is denoted by |S|.
II. PROBLEM FORMULATION
We consider a discrete-time uncertain heterogeneous MAS
in the form
x+
i = ¯Aixi + ¯Biui + Eiv
ei = yi −Fv = ¯Cixi + ¯Diui −Fv,
i = 1, . . . , N
(1)
where xi ∈Rni is the state, ui ∈Rmi is the control input,
and ei ∈Rp is the tracking error of subsystem i. Moreover,
¯Ai = Ai + δAi, ¯Bi = Bi + δBi, ¯Ci = Ci + δCi, and ¯Di =
Di+δDi are uncertain matrices with Ai, Bi, Ci, and Di being
their nominal parts. The results of this article hold for any
matrices Ei and F, which do not need to be known. Also,
v ∈Rn0 is the exogenous signal generated by the exosystem
v+ = A0v.
(2)
This autonomous system yields the reference Fv to be tracked
and the disturbance Eiv to be rejected by subsystem i.
In the context of the RCORP, the subsystems of (1),
considered the followers, and the exosystem (2), considered
the leader, constitute a leader-follower MAS of N + 1 agents.
To model the information exchange between N followers,
we use a time-invariant directed graph G = (N, E) without
self-loops, where N
= {1, . . . , N} is the node set and
E ⊆{(i, j) | i, j ∈N, i ̸= j} is the edge set. Here, node
i ∈N corresponds to follower i, and for each j, i ∈N,
we put (j, i) ∈E if, and only if, follower i has access to
the information of follower j. The entries of the adjacency
matrix A = [aij] ∈RN×N of the graph G are determined
by the rule that for each j, i ∈N, aij > 0 if (j, i) ∈E
and aij = 0 otherwise. The in-degree di of follower i is
defined as di = PN
j=1 aij. Furthermore, the leader is included
in the information exchange model by augmenting the graph
G as follows: Let ¯G = ( ¯
N, ¯E) be a directed graph with
¯
N = N ∪{0}, ¯E = E ∪E′, where E′ ⊊{(0, i) | i ∈N}.
Here, node 0 corresponds to the leader, and for any i ∈N,
we put (0, i) ∈E′ if, and only if, follower i has access to the
leader’s information. For any i ∈N, the pinning gain gi > 0 if
(0, i) ∈E′ and gi = 0 otherwise. A control law that relies on
the information exchange modeled by the augmented directed
graph ¯G is called a distributed control law.
The tracking error ei is available to a proper subset of
followers. Each follower also has access to the relative output
yi −yj = ei −ej for some j ∈N, depending on G. Based
on the available information, a local virtual tracking error for
each follower is defined as
evi =
1
di + gi


N
X
j=1
aij(ei −ej) + giei


(3)
where we assume di + gi > 0 for all i ∈N in this article1.
Consider the distributed dynamic state feedback control law
z+
i = G1izi + G2ievi
ui = K1ixi + K2izi,
i = 1, . . . , N
(4)
where zi ∈Rnz is the controller state of follower i. The
matrices G1i ∈Rnz×nz, G2i ∈Rnz×p, K1i ∈Rmi×ni, and
K2i ∈Rmi×nz are control gains to be designed.
For convenience, the MAS uncertainty is represented with
δ =


[vec(δA1)T, . . . , vec(δAN)T]T
[vec(δB1)T, . . . , vec(δBN)T]T
[vec(δC1)T, . . . , vec(δCN)T]T
[vec(δD1)T, . . . , vec(δDN)T]T

.
1Condition 1 is sufficient for this assumption to hold, but it is not necessary
in general. It becomes necessary when the directed graph G is acyclic.


--- Page 3 ---
3
The closed-loop system consists of (1), (3), and (4). It is re-
ferred to as the nominal closed-loop system if δ = 0. We now
state the RCORP for discrete-time uncertain heterogeneous
MASs. Its definitions for continuous-time and discrete-time
MASs with identical nominal follower dynamics can be found
in Definition 1 of [7] and Problem 1 of [19], respectively.
Problem 1 (RCORP): Given the MAS composed of (1) and
(2), and the augmented directed graph ¯G, find a distributed
control law of the form (4) such that
(i) The nominal closed-loop system matrix is Schur2;
(ii) For i ∈N, for any xi(0), zi(0), v(0), Ei, and F, and
for any δ ∈∆, where ∆is a set of δ such that the
closed-loop system matrix is Schur, limt→∞ei(t) = 0.
Remark 1: It is well known that if the nominal closed-
loop system matrix is Schur, then the set ∆contains 0 in
its interior. Thus, only the nominal closed-loop system matrix
is considered in property (i) of Problem 1.
III. SOLVABILITY OF THE RCORP
For the solvability of Problem 1, we will refer to the
following conditions.
Condition 1: The augmented directed graph ¯G contains a
directed spanning tree.
Condition 2: For any λ ∈spec(A0), |λ| ≥1.
Condition 3: For any i ∈N, the pair (G1i, G2i) incorpo-
rates a p-copy internal model of A03.
Condition 4: For any i ∈N,
rank
Ai −λIni
Bi
Ci
Di

= ni + p
∀λ ∈spec(A0).
Condition 5: For any i ∈N, the pair (Ai, Bi) is stabilizable.
Conditions 2–5 are standard in linear output regulation
theory (e.g., see Chapter 1 in [3]), and so is Condition 1 while
tackling the RCORP (e.g., see [7], [8]).
Considering (1)–(4), the closed-loop system and the exosys-
tem can be compactly written as
x+
g = ¯Agxg + Bgva
v+
a = A0ava
e = ¯Cgxg + Dgva
(5)
with xg = [xT, zT]T, x = [xT
1 , . . . , xT
N]T, z = [zT
1 , . . . , zT
N]T,
va = 1N ⊗v, e = [eT
1 , . . . , eT
N]T, and the following matrices
¯Ag = ¯A + ¯BK, K =

diag(K1i)
diag(K2i)

¯A =

diag( ¯Ai)
0
diag(G2i)Wdiag( ¯Ci)
diag(G1i)

¯B =

diag( ¯Bi)
diag(G2i)Wdiag( ¯Di)

, Bg =

diag(Ei)
−diag(G2i)WFa

¯Cg =
diag( ¯Ci + ¯DiK1i)
diag( ¯DiK2i)
Dg = −Fa, Fa = IN ⊗F, A0a = IN ⊗A0
F = diag((di + gi)−1), W = (IN −FA) ⊗Ip.
(6)
2That is, all its eigenvalues have modulus less than 1.
3The pair (G1i, G2i) takes the following form: G1i = diag(αℓi) and
G2i = diag(βℓi), where for ℓ= 1, . . . , p, αℓi is a square matrix and βℓi
is a column vector such that the minimal polynomial of A0 is equal to the
characteristic polynomial of αℓi and the pair (αℓi, βℓi) is reachable.
The nominal closed-loop system and output matrices are
denoted by Ag and Cg, respectively. The matrix Ag can be
written as Ag = A+BK, where A and B denote the nominal
(i.e., δ = 0) parts of ¯A and ¯B, respectively.
We now tailor Lemma 7.3 in [4] for the system (5).
Lemma 1: If Ag is Schur and for any diag(Ei), F, and
for any δ ∈∆, where ∆= {δ | ¯Ag is Schur}, there exists a
matrix Xg such that
XgA0a = ¯AgXg + Bg
0 = ¯CgXg + Dg
(7)
then Problem 1 is solved.
The linear matrix equations (LMEs) (7), which depend
on the unknown matrices diag(Ei), F, and the uncertain
parameter δ, sets an algebraic goal of the distributed control
law (4). To meet this goal, we use the following two lemmas.
Lemma 2 (Lemma 4.1 and Remark 4.1 in [10]): Under
Condition 1, W is nonsingular.
Lemma 3: Let Condition 3 hold. If there exists a pair
(Yg, Zg) such that
ZgA0a = diag(G1i)Zg + diag(G2i)Yg
(8)
then Yg = 0.
Proof: Due to the block-diagonal structure of A0a, where
each block on the diagonal is A0, the minimal polynomials
for A0a and A0 are the same. Thus, under Condition 3, the
pair (diag(G1i), diag(G2i)) incorporates an Np-copy internal
model of A0a. By Lemma 7.4 in [4], the proof is over.
Lemma 4.3 in [10] has extended Lemma 1.27 of [3],
which is key for p-copy internal model-based control of
single systems, to internal model-based distributed control
of continuous-time heterogeneous (in dimension) MASs over
general directed graphs. Within the context of RCORP, we
present the following key lemma, the discrete-time counterpart
of the result in [10] for the distributed control law (4).
Lemma 4: Let Conditions 1–3 hold. For any diag(Ei), F,
and for any δ ∈∆, there exists a unique matrix Xg that
satisfies the LMEs (7).
Proof: Fix diag(Ei), F, and let δ ∈∆. Then ¯Ag is Schur.
Due to the structure of A0a, spec(A0a) = spec(A0). Under
Condition 2, A0a and ¯Ag have no eigenvalues in common.
Thus, the Sylvester equation (i.e., the first LME) in (7) has
a unique solution Xg by Proposition A.2 in [3]. In addition,
we show that the matrix Xg satisfies the second LME in (7).
To this end, partition Xg as Xg = [XT ZT]T, where X ∈
R¯n×Nn0, Z ∈RNnz×Nn0, and ¯n = PN
i=1 ni. The Sylvester
equation can now be expanded as
XA0a = diag( ¯Ai + ¯BiK1i)X + diag( ¯BiK2i)Z + diag(Ei)
ZA0a = diag(G1i)Z + diag(G2i)WY
(9)
where
Y = ¯CgXg + Dg.
Observe that the second equation in (9) is in the form of (8).
Under Condition 3, we infer from Lemma 3 that WY = 0.
Under Condition 1, by Lemma 2, W is nonsingular. Therefore,
WY = 0 implies Y = 0. This completes the proof.


--- Page 4 ---
4
The following theorem, which is the discrete-time version
of Theorem 4.1 in [10], provides sufficient conditions for the
solvability of the RCORP.
Theorem 1: Let Conditions 1–3 hold. If Ag is Schur, then
Problem 1 is solved.
Proof: The proof follows from Lemmas 1 and 4.
Remark 2: The first equation in (4), called the distributed p-
copy internal model of A0 under Condition 3 (e.g., see Chapter
9 in [4]), leads to the augmented pair (A, B). Theorem 1
tells us that under Conditions 1–3, to solve Problem 1, it is
sufficient to solve the following stabilization problem: Design
the structured K in (6) such that Ag = A + BK is Schur.
Although the stabilizability of the pair (A, B) is necessary for
such a K to exist, as Example 1 shows, it is not sufficient due
to the zero entries of K.
Under mild conditions, the following lemma establishes the
stabilizability of the pair (A, B). Compared to the single sys-
tem case (e.g., see Lemma 1.37 in [3]), the graph connectivity
condition, Condition 1, is the only additional one.
Lemma 5: Under Conditions 1 and 3–5, the pair (A, B) is
stabilizable.
Proof: Define M(λ) = [A −λI¯n+Nnz B], where ¯n =
PN
i=1 ni. Let λ ∈C such that |λ| ≥1. According to the
PBH test for stabilizability, it suffices to show rankM(λ) =
¯n + Nnz. We first consider the case that λ /∈spec(A0).
Under Condition 3, spec(diag(G1i)) = spec(A0). There-
fore, det(diag(G1i) −λINnz) ̸= 0. Under Condition 5,
(diag(Ai), diag(Bi)) is stabilizable. Thus, by the PBH test,
rank[diag(Ai) −λI¯n diag(Bi)] = ¯n. One can now conclude
that rankM(λ) = ¯n + Nnz.
It remains to consider the case that λ ∈spec(A0). We can
write M(λ) = M1(λ)M2(λ), where
M1(λ) =
I¯n
0
0
0
diag(G2i)W
diag(G1i) −λINnz

M2(λ) =


diag(Ai) −λI¯n
0
diag(Bi)
diag(Ci)
0
diag(Di)
0
INnz
0

.
Under Condition 3, the pair (diag(G1i), diag(G2i)) is reach-
able. By the reachability matrix test, rankΓ = Nnz, where
Γ =

diag(G2i)
diag(G1iG2i)
. . .
diag(GNnz−1
1i
G2i)

.
Under Condition 1, by Lemma 2, W is nonsingular. Then
INnz ⊗W is nonsingular. In conjunction with rankΓ =
Nnz, this implies rankΓ(INnz ⊗W) = Nnz. Observe
that Γ(INnz ⊗W) is the reachability matrix of the pair
(diag(G1i), diag(G2i)W). By the reachability matrix test, the
pair (diag(G1i), diag(G2i)W) is reachable. From the PBH
test, rank[diag(G1i)−λINnz diag(G2i)W] = Nnz. It is now
clear from the structure of M1(λ) that rankM1(λ) = ¯n+Nnz.
Under Condition 4, it is also clear from the structure of M2(λ)
that rankM2(λ) = ¯n + Nnz + Np. Due to the size of
M(λ), rankM(λ) ≤¯n+Nnz. But, by Sylvester’s inequality,
rankM(λ) ≥¯n + Nnz Hence, rankM(λ) = ¯n + Nnz.
Example 1: Let Ai = 0.5, Bi = 0, and Ci = Di = 1 for
i = 1, 2, and A0 = 10. Consider the adjacency matrix A with
a21 = a12 = 1, and the pinning gains g1 = 1 and g2 = 0.
Select G1i = G2i = 10 for i = 1, 2. Note that Conditions 1–5
hold. By Lemma 5, the pair (A, B) is stabilizable. Through
structured K in (6), the eigenvalues of A + BK are λ1 =
λ2 = 0.5, λ3 = 5(s + q) + 10, and λ4 = 5(s −q) + 10,
where s = K21 + K22 and q =
p
K2
21 + K2
22. By Cauchy-
Schwarz inequality, one can show that |s|/
√
2 ≤q. Suppose
for a contradiction that there exist K21 and K22 such that
A + BK is Schur. Then |λ3| = |5(s + q) + 10| < 1 and
|λ4| = |5(s −q) + 10| < 1. Equivalently,
−2.2 < s + q < −1.8,
−2.2 < s −q < −1.8.
These two inequalities imply s ∈(−2.2, −1.8). Since q ≥0,
they also imply q ∈[0, 0.2). By considering |s|/
√
2 ≤q, 1.8 <
|s|, and q < 0.2, we obtain 1.8/
√
2 < 0.2, a contradiction.
Therefore, there is no structured K that renders A+BK Schur
even though the pair (A, B) is stabilizable.
IV. STRUCTURED GLOBAL DESIGN OF K
Under Conditions 1–3, solving Problem 1 reduces to ful-
fillment of its property (i), as highlighted in Remark 2. It
is well known that Ag is Schur if, and only if, there exists
a P ≻0 satisfying the discrete-time Lyapunov inequality,
also referred to as the Stein inequality, AT
g PAg −P ≺0.
Since spec(Ag) = spec(AT
g ), Ag is Schur if, and only if,
there exists a P ≻0 satisfying the dual of the discrete time
Lyapunov inequality AgPAT
g −P ≺0. The dual inequality is
also called the Lyapunov inequality. Given K, both inequality
constraint functions are convex in P. However, none of them
are jointly convex in K and P. Due to the structure of
K, unlike control gains without any structure, we cannot
directly obtain an equivalent LMI through a simple change
of variables. On the other hand, imposing a structure on P
turns the Lyapunov inequality into a structured Lyapunov
inequality. This makes the change of variables applicable and
yields a convex formulation for the synthesis of the structured
control gain K. The idea and its consequences are formulated
in Lemma 6 and Theorem 2, which are the discrete-time
counterparts of Lemma 3 and Theorem 1 in [8].
Lemma 6: There exist matrices K in the form of (6) and
P =
diag(P1i)
diag(Poi)
diag(P T
oi)
diag(P2i)

≻0
(10)
where P1i ∈Sni
++, P2i ∈Snz
++ necessarily, and Poi ∈Rni×nz
for all i ∈N, such that
AgPAT
g −P ≺0
(11)
if, and only if, there exist matrices
Q =
diag(Q1i)
diag(Qoi)
diag(QT
oi)
diag(Q2i)

≻0
Y =
diag(Y1i)
diag(Y2i)
(12)
where Q1i ∈Sni
++, Q2i ∈Snz
++ necessarily, Qoi ∈Rni×nz
Y1i ∈Rmi×ni, and Y2i ∈Rmi×nz for all i ∈N, such that

−Q
AQ + BY
QAT + Y TBT
−Q

≺0.
(13)


--- Page 5 ---
5
Proof: For any Q ≻0 and Y of appropriate sizes, the
inequality (13) is equivalent to
(A + BY Q−1)Q(A + BY Q−1)T −Q ≺0
(14)
by the Schur complement of −Q in the second diagonal block
of the matrix in (13). To show the “if” part, suppose there exist
Q and Y in the form of (12) such that the inequality (13) holds.
It can be verified by Theorem 2.1 in [26] that Q−1 is in the
form of Q. Hence, Y Q−1 satisfies the structure of K in (6).
Since (13) is equivalent to (14), the Lyapunov inequality (11)
holds with P = Q and K = Y Q−1. To show the “only if”
part, assume there exist K in the form of (6) and P in the
form of (10) such that the Lyapunov inequality (11) holds.
Noting KP satisfies the structure of Y in (12), the inequality
(14) holds with Q = P and Y = KP. The proof is thus
completed by the equivalence of (14) and (13).
Remark 3: To interpret this result in terms of stabilizability,
the stabilizability of the pair (A, B) through structured K in
(6) will be referred to as the structured stabilizability of the
pair (A, B). The Lyapunov inequality (11) with structured
P in the form of (10) will be referred to as the structured
Lyapunov inequality. Though the LMI in Lemma 6 does not
characterize the structured stabilizability of the pair (A, B)
due to the restriction of P to the form in (10), it characterizes
the structured stabilizability of the pair (A, B) with respect to
the structured Lyapunov inequality.
Remark 4: With the following permutation matrix
T =

diag

Ini
0nzi×ni

diag
0ni×nzi
Inzi

(15)
where nzi = nz for all i ∈N, the matrix Q in the form of
(12) is permutation similar to the matrix
diag
Q1i
Qoi
QT
oi
Q2i

.
Since Q ≻0, this implies that, for any i ∈N,
Q1i
Qoi
QT
oi
Q2i

≻0.
Therefore, the matrix inverse in (16) is well-defined.
Theorem 2: If there exist Q and Y in the form of (12)
such that (13) holds, and if the control gains K1i and K2i are
recovered as
K1i
K2i

=
Y1i
Y2i
 Q1i
Qoi
QT
oi
Q2i
−1
(16)
for all i ∈N, then Ag is Schur. If, in addition, Conditions
1–3 hold, then Problem 1 is solved.
Proof: By (16), Y1i = K1iQ1i + K2iQT
oi and Y2i =
K1iQoi + K2iQ2i for all i ∈N. This implies that Y = KQ,
where K satisfies the structure in (6). From the proof of the
“if” part of Lemma 6, the Lyapunov inequality (11) holds with
P = Q and K. This proves the first statement of the theorem.
The second statement follows from Theorem 1.
Remark 5: Through the structured Lyapunov inequality, the
control gains K1i and K2i of each follower are recovered
in (16) if the LMI in Lemma 6 is feasible. This convex
semidefinite feasibility program can be reliably solved using
fast solvers with guaranteed polynomial-time convergence
[27]–[29].
V. AGENT-WISE LOCAL DESIGN OF K
With the proposed structured global design in Section
IV, the synthesis of the control gains K1i and K2i for all
i ∈N becomes a convex program, and hence, computation-
ally tractable. However, the synthesis is centralized (i.e., an
authority must know the nominal parameters of all followers),
even though the implementation of the control law (4) remains
distributed. To design each follower’s control gains K1i and
K2i independently of the other followers, this section focuses
on the individual nominal dynamics of each follower.
Considering (1)–(4) and letting v(0) = 0, we arrive at the
exosystem-free nominal local dynamics of followers given by
ξ+
i = Afiξi + Bfiµi
ei = Cfiξi,
i = 1, . . . , N
with ξi = [xT
i zT
i ]T, µi = (di + gi)−1 PN
j=1 aijej, and the
following matrices
Afi = Aoi + BoiKi, Ki =
K1i
K2i

Aoi =
 Ai
0
G2iCi
G1i

, Boi =
 Bi
G2iDi

, Bfi =

0
−G2i

Cfi = Coi + DiKi, Coi =
Ci
0
.
Since |E′| < N and di + gi > 0 for all i ∈N, we have
rank(FA) > 0. Hence, FA has at least one nonzero singular
value. Let σmin(FA) and σmax(FA) denote the minimum
nonzero singular value and maximum singular value of FA.
The following theorem now derives agent-wise local sufficient
conditions for the matrix Ag to be Schur.
Theorem 3: If, for any i ∈N, there exist Ki, Pi ≻0, and
ri ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA) such that
AfiPiAT
fi −Pi + riBfi(Ip + CfiPiCT
fi)BT
fi
AfiPiCT
fi
CfiPiAT
fi
−Ip

≺0
σmin(FA)Ip ⪯CfiPiCT
fi ⪯σmax(FA)Ip
(17)
then Ag is Schur. If, in addition, Conditions 1–3 hold, then
Problem 1 is solved.
Proof: For any Ki and symmetric Pi of appropriate sizes,
and for any ri ∈R, the inequalities (17) are equivalent to
Mi ≜AfiPiAT
fi −Pi + AfiPiCT
fiCfiPiAT
fi + riBfiBT
fi
+ riBfiCfiPiCT
fiBT
fi ≺0
σmin(FA) ≤λmin(CfiPiCT
fi)
λmax(CfiPiCT
fi) ≤σmax(FA)
(18)
by the Schur complement of −Ip in the matrix of the first
inequality in (17) and the symmetry of CfiPiCT
fi. Thus, to
prove the lemma, for any i ∈N, let Ki, Pi ≻0, and ri ≥
σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA) be such that the inequalities (18) hold.
For any i ∈N, partition Pi as
Pi =
P1i
Poi
P T
oi
P2i

(19)
where P1i ∈Sni
++, P2i ∈Snz
++ necessarily, and Poi ∈Rni×nz.
With the permutation matrix (15), define P = T Tdiag(Pi)T.
Note that T T = T −1. Therefore, P is permutation similar to
diag(Pi). Since Pi ≻0 for all i ∈N, this ensures that P is in


--- Page 6 ---
6
the form of (10). To show that the Lyapunov inequality (11)
holds, we similarly define M = T Tdiag(Mi)T. In conjunction
with the first two lines of (18), this yields
M = ˆAP ˆAT −P + ˆAPCT
g CgP ˆAT + ˆB(R ⊗Ip) ˆBT
+ ˆB(R1/2 ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (R1/2 ⊗Ip) ˆBT ≺0
(20)
where
ˆA =

diag(Ai + BiK1i)
diag(BiK2i)
diag(G2i(Ci + DiK1i))
diag(G1i + G2iDiK2i)

ˆB =

0
−diag(G2i)

, R = diag(ri).
Note that Cg = diag(Cfi)T. It can be easily verified that
CgPCT
g = diag(CfiPiCT
fi). Hence,
spec(CgPCT
g ) =
N
[
i=1
spec(CfiPiCT
fi).
Since σmin(FA) ≤λmin(CfiPiCT
fi) and λmax(CfiPiCT
fi) ≤
σmax(FA) for all i ∈N, this, together with the symmetry of
CgPCT
g , implies
σmin(FA) ≤λmin(CgPCT
g ) ≤λmax(CgPCT
g ) ≤σmax(FA).
From these inequalities, we have
λmax(CgPCT
g )
λmin(CgPCT
g ) ≤σmax(FA)
σmin(FA)
which yields
σ2
max(FA)λmax(CgPCT
g )
λmin(CgPCT
g ) ≤ri,
i = 1, . . . , N
(21)
because ri ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA) for all i ∈N. Due to
the symmetry of CgPCT
g , we have
λmin(CgPCT
g )INp ⪯CgPCT
g ⪯λmax(CgPCT
g )INp.
(22)
By the first inequality in (22), we obtain
λmin(CgPCT
g )(R ⊗Ip) ⪯(R1/2 ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (R1/2 ⊗Ip).
Through (21), this yields
σ2
max(FA)λmax(CgPCT
g )INp ⪯
(R1/2 ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (R1/2 ⊗Ip).
(23)
From the second inequality in (22), we have
(FA ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (FA ⊗Ip)T ⪯
λmax(CgPCT
g )(FAATFT ⊗Ip).
(24)
Moreover, we have the following set equalities
spec(FAATFT ⊗Ip) = spec(FAATFT)
= spec(ATFTFA)
(25)
where the first equality follows from Proposition 7.1.10 in
[30] and the second holds since FA is a square matrix. In
conjunction with the symmetry of FAATFT⊗Ip, (25) implies
FAATFT ⊗Ip ⪯σ2
max(FA)INp.
(26)
We now infer from (23), (24), and (26) that
(FA ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (FA ⊗Ip)T ⪯
(R1/2 ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (R1/2 ⊗Ip).
(27)
Since ri ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA) for all i ∈N, one can
derive from (26) that
FAATFT ⊗Ip ⪯R ⊗Ip.
(28)
By Lemma 6.2 in [31], for any real matrices U and V of
appropriate sizes, UV +V TU T ⪯UU T +V TV . Letting U =
ˆB(FA ⊗Ip) and V = CgP ˆAT gives
ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)CgP ˆAT + ˆAPCT
g (FA ⊗Ip)T ˆBT ⪯
ˆB(FAATFT ⊗Ip) ˆBT + ˆAPCT
g CgP ˆAT.
(29)
Utilizing (27)–(29), we obtain from (20) that
ˆAP ˆAT + ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)CgP ˆAT + ˆAPCT
g (FA ⊗Ip)T ˆBT
+ ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)CgPCT
g (FA ⊗Ip)T ˆBT −P ≺0
which can be equivalently written as
( ˆA + ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)Cg)P( ˆA + ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)Cg)T −P ≺0.
Noting Ag = ˆA + ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)Cg, this establishes that Ag is
Schur. The second statement follows from Theorem 1.
Remark 6: Given ri ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA) and Ki, the
inequalities (17) are LMIs in Pi ≻0. However, the feasible set
becomes nonconvex when Ki is treated as a decision variable.
The following corollary presents a convexification that
yields a convex semidefinite feasibility program for each
follower to synthesize Ki when Di = 0.
Corollary 1: Let Di = 0 and ri ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA)
for all i ∈N. If, for any i ∈N, there exist Pi ≻0, Yi, and
Θi such that
 Θi
Yi
Y T
i
Pi

⪰0

Ωi
(AoiPi + BoiYi)CT
oi
Coi(PiAT
oi + Y T
i BT
oi)
−Ip

≺0
σmin(FA)Ip ⪯CoiPiCT
oi ⪯σmax(FA)Ip
(30)
where Θi ⪰0 necessarily and
Ωi = AoiPiAT
oi + BoiYiAT
oi + AoiY T
i BT
oi + BoiΘiBT
oi −Pi
+ riBfiBT
fi + riBfiCoiPiCT
oiBT
fi
and if the control gain Ki is recovered as Ki = YiP −1
i
for all
i ∈N, then Ag is Schur. If, in addition, Conditions 1–3 hold,
then Problem 1 is solved.
Proof: Fix i ∈N. Clearly, it suffices to show that the first
inequality in (18) holds. Since Ki = YiP −1
i
, Yi = KiPi. Thus,
the Schur complement of Pi in the matrix of the first inequality
in (30) gives KiPiKT
i ⪯Θi. Since Di = 0, Cfi = Coi. Using
Cfi = Coi, KiPiKT
i ⪯Θi, and Yi = KiPi, we arrive at the
following inequality
AfiPiAT
fi −Pi + riBfiBT
fi + riBfiCfiPiCT
fiBT
fi ⪯Ωi. (31)


--- Page 7 ---
7
Using Cfi = Coi and Yi = KiPi, from the second inequality
in (30), we have
AfiPiCT
fiCfiPiAT
fi + Ωi ≺0.
This and (31) now yield the first inequality in (18).
As in the continuous-time case (see [32] and Remark 4.5 in
[10]), the next corollary tells that the design of Ki for each
follower simplifies to making the nominal local system matrix
Afi Schur if the directed graph G is acyclic. The existence of
such a Ki is ensured under Conditions 3–5 (e.g., see Lemma
1.37 in [3]), and linear control design techniques, such as
eigenvalue assignment and LQR, can be used to find one.
Corollary 2: Let the directed graph G be acyclic. Then the
following statements are true:
(i) The matrix Ag is Schur if, and only if, Afi is Schur for
all i ∈N.
(ii) Let Conditions 2 and 3 hold. If Afi is Schur for all
i ∈N, then Problem 1 is solved.
Proof: The second statement follows from the first foot-
note, the first statement of the corollary, and Theorem 1.
Therefore, it suffices to prove the first statement. We recall
from the proof of Theorem 3 that Ag = ˆA + ˆB(FA ⊗Ip)Cg.
Let Ac = diag(Afi) + diag(Bfi)(FA ⊗Ip)diag(Cfi). With
the permutation matrix (15), we have diag(Afi) = T ˆAT T,
diag(Bfi) = T ˆB, and diag(Cfi) = CgT T. Thus, TAgT T =
Ac, which implies that Ag is similar to Ac. Since the directed
graph G is acyclic, one can relabel the nodes in N, and hence
the followers, such that i > j if (j, i) ∈E. Then, without
loss of generality, we assume that the adjacency matrix A
is lower triangular. Since the diagonal entries of A are zero
and F is a diagonal matrix, FA is a lower triangular matrix
with zero diagonal entries. Consequently, Ac is a block lower
triangular matrix and its diagonal blocks are Af1, . . . , AfN.
In conjunction with Ag being similar to Ac, this implies that
spec(Ag) = spec(diag(Afi)). Hence, the proof is over.
VI. SET INCLUSIONS AMONG CONTROL GAIN SETS
EMERGING FROM GLOBAL AND LOCAL PERSPECTIVES
The results in Sections IV and V yield several sets of
control gains that make Ag Schur from both structured global
and agent-wise local perspectives. Including the universal set
denoted by KG, we investigate the relationships between these
sets. To this end, the control gain sets of interest are given by
KG = {K | (11) is feasible for some P ≻0}
KS = {K | (11) is feasible for some P subject to (10)}
KLA = {K | Afi is Schur for all i ∈N}
KLC =


K

(17) is feasible for some ri ≥σ3
max(FA)
σmin(FA)
and Pi ≻0, for all i ∈N


.
Corollary 3: The set inclusions KLC ⊆KS ⊆KG hold.
Proof: The inclusion KLC ⊆KS follows from the proof
of Theorem 3 and the inclusion KS ⊆KG is by definition.
One may also question whether the following inclusions
hold: (i) KG ⊆KS, (ii) KS ⊆KLC, (iii) KLA ⊆KG, and
(iv) KG ⊆KLA. As shown in Examples 2–5, the answer is
negative for each case.
Example 2: Let Ai = 0 and Bi = Ci = Di = 1 for
i = 1, 2, and A0 = 2. Consider the same adjacency matrix
and the pinning gains given in Example 1. Select G1i = 2 and
G2i = 1 for i = 1, 2. With K11 = 1, K12 = −0.9, K21 = −1,
and K22 = −2, Ag is Schur, and hence, K ∈KG. Suppose
for a contradiction that K ∈KS. Then there exists a structured
P in the form of (10) such that (11) holds. Thus, P1i > 0 and
P2i > 0 for i = 1, 2, and
Po1 > 0.5P21
P22 < 0.0475P12 −0.9Po2
P22 < 0.1Po2 −0.0025P12 −4P11 −4Po1
P22 > P21 + 0.01P12 + 4P11 −4Po1
(32)
where the inequalities (32) follow from the diagonal entries of
AgPAT
g −P being negative. Since P21 > 0, the first inequality
in (32) implies Po1 > 0. Since P1i > 0 for i = 1, 2, P22 > 0,
and Po1 > 0, we infer from the third inequality in (32) that
Po2 > 0. The second inequality in (32) is equivalent to
0.0125P12 > P22/3.8 + 0.9Po2/3.8.
(33)
From the third and fourth inequalities in (32), we obtain
0.0125P12 < 0.1Po2 −8P11 −P21.
(34)
We now deduce from (33) and (34) that −0.52Po2 > P22 +
30.4P11 + 3.8P21. In conjunction with P11 > 0, P2i > 0 for
i = 1, 2, and Po2 > 0, this implies that 0 > 0, a contradiction.
Hence, KG ⊈KS.
Example 3: Let Ai = 0.5, Bi = 0, and Ci = Di = 1 for
i = 1, 2, and A0 = 1. Consider the adjacency matrix A with
a21 = 1, a12 = 0, and the pinning gains g1 = 1 and g2 = 0.
Choose G1i = G2i = 1 for i = 1, 2. Take K1i = −1 for
i = 1, 2, K21 = −0.5, and K22 = −1. Then (11) is feasible
for P = I4, and hence, K ∈KS. Suppose for a contradiction
that K ∈KLC. Then there exist r1 ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA)
and P1 ≻0, with the partition in (19), such that the inequalities
(17) hold. Note that σmax(FA) = σmin(FA) = 1. Thus,
r1 ≥1, and the last two inequalities in (17) imply that P21 =
4. Since the first inequality in (17) is equivalent to the first
inequality in (18), M1 ≺0. Thus, 2r1 −2, which is a diagonal
entry of M1, must be negative. In conjunction with r1 ≥1,
this implies that 1 < 1, a contradiction. Hence, KS ⊈KLC.
Considering this and the first inclusion in Corollary 3, we
conclude that KLC is a proper subset of KS for this example.
Example 4: Consider Example 1. Take K = −[I2 I2]. Then
Afi is Schur for i = 1, 2, but Ag is not. Thus, KLA ⊈KG.
Example 5: Consider Example 2. Even though K ∈KG,
K /∈KLA since Af1 is not Schur. Thus, KG ⊈KLA. Note also
that Conditions 1–3 hold. It is now worth highlighting that, by
Theorem 1, Problem 1 is still solved without Af1 being Schur.
Corollary 4: If G is acyclic, then KLA = KG.
Proof: It is immediate from Corollary 2 (i).
Finally, the following example demonstrates the agent-wise
local synthesis of a K ∈KLC that solves Problem 1 for a
heterogeneous MAS by utilizing Corollary 1.
Example 6: Let Ai = Bi = Ci = 1, i = 1, 3,
Ai =
1
1
0
1

, Bi =
0.5
1

, Ci =
1
0
,
i = 2, 4


--- Page 8 ---
8
Di = 0, i = 1, 2, 3, 4, and A0 = 1. Consider the adjacency
matrix A with a21 = a12 = a32 = 0.2, a14 = a24 = a34 =
a23 = 0.1, and the pinning gains g1 = 0.5, g4 = 0.1. The
remaining entries of A and the rest of the pinning gains are
zero. Select G1i = G2i = 1 for i = 1, 2, 3, 4. Note that Con-
ditions 1–3 hold. Choose ri = 0.92 ≥σ3
max(FA)/σmin(FA)
for i = 1, 2, 3, 4. To solve (30), we use CVX a package for
modeling and solving convex programs [33], and obtain
Pi =
 0.7231
−1.8112
−1.8112
20.7015

Yi =
−0.7377
−0.0532
, Θi = 1.2077, i = 1, 3
Pi =


0.7033
−0.8646
−1.7348
−0.8646
6.2609
−0.0550
−1.7348
−0.0550
17.0840


Yi =

0.5105
−8.4232
0.1100

, Θi = 12.8017, i = 2, 4.
Each follower’s control gain Ki is recovered as suggested in
Corollary 1, and is given below
Ki =
−1.3147
−0.1176
, i = 1, 3
Ki =

−1.5978
−1.5674
−0.1609

, i = 2, 4.
By the proof of Corollary 1, K ∈KLC. We also conclude
from Corollary 1 that Problem 1 is solved.
VII. CONCLUSION
We studied the solvability of the RCORP of discrete-time
uncertain heterogeneous linear MASs through the distributed
internal model approach. Global and agent-wise local design
methods, involving the feasibility of LMIs, were presented.
The global one is less conservative, whereas the local is more
scalable. The solvability result and design methods in this
article can serve as a building block for research in data-
driven distributed control, for instance, in extending the data
informativity approach for robust output regulation [34] and
the data-driven cooperative output regulation [35] to RCORP.
REFERENCES
[1] E. Davison, “The robust control of a servomechanism problem for linear
time-invariant multivariable systems,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control,
vol. 21, no. 1, pp. 25–34, 1976.

theory,” Automatica, vol. 12, no. 5, pp. 457–465, 1976.
[3] J. Huang, Nonlinear output regulation: Theory and applications. SIAM,
2004.
[4] H. Cai, Y. Su, and J. Huang, Cooperative control of multi-agent sys-
tems: Distributed observer and distributed internal model approaches.
Springer, 2022.

via distributed observer and distributed internal model approaches with
application to networked quadrotors and mobile robots,” in AIAA SciTech
Forum, 2026, p. 2201.

K. M. Dogan, “Containment control of multi-human multi-agent systems
under time delays,” IEEE Trans. Syst., Man, Cybern. Syst., vol. 54, no. 6,
pp. 3344–3356, 2024.

output regulation for linear uncertain multi-agent systems,” IEEE Trans.
Autom. Control, vol. 58, no. 5, pp. 1275–1279, 2013.

distributed control synthesis approach,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control,
vol. 66, no. 9, pp. 4289–4296, 2021.

heterogeneous uncertain linear agents,” Automatica, vol. 119, p. 109094,
2020.

heterogeneous linear multiagent systems,” Int. J. Control, vol. 94, no. 5,
pp. 1402–1414, 2021.

B. Ac¸ıkmes¸e, “Regional eigenvalue assignment in cooperative linear
output regulation,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control, vol. 68, no. 7, pp. 4265–
4272, 2023.

design problems,” SIAM J. Control Optim., vol. 35, no. 6, pp. 2118–
2127, 1997.

of discrete-time non-linear systems: The LMI approach,” Int. J. Control,
vol. 74, no. 9, pp. 873–879, 2001.

179, 2005.

computation.

tions,” Linear Algebra Appl., vol. 492, pp. 38–51, 2016.

block-diagonal solutions to Lyapunov and H∞Riccati inequalities,”
IEEE Trans. Autom. Control, vol. 65, no. 7, pp. 3170–3175, 2020.

stabilizers for LTI systems,” IEEE Control Syst. Lett., vol. 4, no. 2, pp.
289–294, 2020.

regulation of discrete-time linear multi-agent systems,” IEEE/CAA J.
Autom. Sin., vol. 1, no. 2, pp. 204–209, 2014.

for discrete-time linear time-delay multi-agent systems,” Int. J. Robust

agent systems with system uncertainties,” Appl. Intell., vol. 50, no. 6,
pp. 1794–1812, 2020.

of heterogeneous linear multi-agent networks via H∞performance
allocation,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control, vol. 64, no. 2, pp. 683–696,

output regulation over switching graphs,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control,

Math. Appl., vol. 43, no. 1, pp. 119–129, 2002.

University Press, 2004.

Springer Nature, 2018.

Princeton University Press, 2009.
[31] K. Gu, V. L. Kharitonov, and J. Chen, Stability of time-delay systems.
Boston, MA: Birkh¨auser, 2003.
[32] X. Wang, Y. Hong, J. Huang, and Z.-P. Jiang, “A distributed control
approach to a robust output regulation problem for multi-agent linear
systems,” IEEE Trans. Autom. Control, vol. 55, no. 12, pp. 2891–2895,
2010.
[33] M. Grant and S. Boyd, “CVX: Matlab software for disciplined convex
programming, version 2.1,” 2014.
[34] L. Zhu and Z. Chen, “Data informativity for robust output regulation,”
IEEE Trans. Autom. Control, vol. 69, no. 10, pp. 7075–7080, 2024.
[35] G. Fattore and M. E. Valcher, “Distributed output synchronization of
discrete-time multi-agent systems: A data-driven approach,” Automatica,
vol. 185, p. 112789, 2026.
